text: **The Strange Record** (佚名) On Mount Lu, there is a valley known as Kangwang Valley, also referred to as North Ridge in the *Shi Leifu Zhi*. There is a city here called Zhaocun. Whenever it is about to rain, one can hear the sounds of drums, horns, flutes, and pipes from the mountain, which gradually approach the city, followed by heavy rain and dark clouds. The local people consider this a regular sign of impending rain. It is also recorded in the *Shi Leifu Zhi* that this was the city of King Kang of Zhou, who was fond of unusual things and traveled to famous mountains, not far away. The city often finds ancient artifacts, large tripods, and weapons like bows, crossbows, and gold, indicating that this is not a place inhabited by ordinary people. The mountain is called "Kangwang" and the city "Zhaocun," suggesting that these names have historical significance. (*Shi Leifu Zhi* 85) On Mount Lu, there are three stone bridges, each tens of meters long but less than a foot wide, and when looked down upon, they appear to have no bottom. During the Xiankang period, Governor Yu of Jiangzhou welcomed Wu Meng, and Wu Meng and his disciples visited the mountain and passed over these bridges. They saw an old man sitting under a ginkgo tree, holding a jade cup filled with sweet dew, which he gave to Wu Meng, who then distributed it to his disciples. They also saw a grand terrace with wide halls, jade palaces and golden rooms, dazzling with precious stones and brilliant colors. There were many precious jade and gold vessels, whose names were unknown. They saw several people talking with Wu Meng, as if they were old acquaintances, and they spent the whole day with the jade ointment. (*Zhu Lin* 31, *Shi Leifu Zhi* 41, 633)

Once upon a time, there was a person who went to Mount Lu to gather pine trees. They heard a voice say, "This cannot be taken." The person followed the sound and climbed up, where they saw a strange flower. Its form was extremely lovely, and its fragrance was extraordinary. Knowing it was divine, they picked it up and consumed it, thereby gaining a lifespan of three hundred years. (Zhu Lin, Chapter Thirty-Six) In the southern wilderness of Nankang, there was Dongwang Mountain. Three villagers climbed to the top of the mountain and found a clear and deep lake, as well as a fruit grove that spanned about four li in circumference. All kinds of fruits were planted in neat rows, with no mixed trees, as if arranged by human effort. The sweet fruits were just ripe, and the three villagers ate together until they were full. They each carried two fruits to show to others, and then began their return. As they searched for the path they had come, they wandered around for half a day, becoming lost. Suddenly, they heard a voice from the air say, "Quickly release the two fruits, and then you may go." The one carrying the fruits was terrified and threw them on the ground. Instantly, they saw the path back, and together they returned home. (Yulian 906, 490; Chuxueji 28; Leiju 86; Shileifu Zhi 27)

There is a deity in Nankang named "Shandu." It has a human-like form, about two feet long, black in color, with red eyes, and yellow hair covering its body. It builds its nest in the deep mountains among trees. The nest shape is like a hard bird egg, according to the broad records, it is shaped like an egg and is about three feet high. Inside is very smooth and brightly colored in five hues. Two pieces are layered together, connected in the middle. The locals say, "The upper part is the male chamber, and the lower part is the female chamber." Around the sides, there are openings like circles. Its body is light and hollow, somewhat resembling a wooden tube. In the center, it is lined with bird feathers. This deity can change and hide itself. Rarely, according to the broad records, can one suddenly see its form. It is likely of the same kind as wood, guest, mountain, ○, etc. Fifteen miles northwest of Gannan County, there is an ancient pond named Yuguang Pond. On it stands a large paulownia tree, about twenty times the girth of a man's arm. The tree is old and hollow inside, and there is a Shandu nest in it. In the first year of the Yuanjia era of the Song Dynasty, two brothers named Yuan Daotong and Yuan Daoxu, who lived in the county, cut down this tree. They took the nest home. The Shandu appeared to the two brothers and said, "I live in the wilderness, what concern is it of yours? There are many large trees that can be used, how can they be counted? The tree has my nest, so you cut it down. Now I will burn your house to avenge your wickedness." At the second watch of the night, fire broke out simultaneously on the inside and outside of the house. The entire house was completely destroyed. Recorded in the *Yuelan* (Imperial View) volume 884 and the *Guangji* (Broad Records) volume 324.

Yudu County in Nankang extends westward along the river, and the *Tang Huiyao* (唐会要) records it as "crossing south of the river." Three miles from the county is a place called Mengkou, where there is a cave that looks like a stone chamber, known as the Mengkou Cave. An old tradition, as mentioned in the *Leeguji* (类聚) volume 90, tells of a divine chicken with a color resembling fine gold. This chicken would emerge from the cave, spread its wings, fly far, and make a long cry. When its sound was heard, it would immediately fly back into the cave. Thus, the stone was named the Golden Chicken Stone. Once, a farmer plowing the side of the mountain saw the chicken come out to play. A tall person then appeared, holding a slingshot and aiming at the chicken. The chicken, seeing the tall person, flew back into the cave. The pebble hit the top of the cave, and the pebble was about six feet in diameter, hanging down and covering the cave, but there was still a small gap that was not large enough for a person to pass through. Another time, a person traveling upstream by boat had not yet reached the cliff several miles away when a person in a yellow robe appeared, carrying two baskets of cucumbers. The person asked to be transported, and the boat owner agreed. The person in the yellow robe then asked for food, and the boat owner gave him a plate of food and wine. After finishing eating, the boat arrived at the base of the cliff. The boat owner then asked for cucumbers, but the person in the yellow robe refused and spat on the plate. The person then climbed up the cliff and entered the stone. Initially, the boat owner was very angry, but when he saw the person enter the stone, he realized the person was supernatural. He then took the plate to look at it and found that the spittle on the plate had turned into gold. This story is also mentioned in the *Ren Fang Shuyi Ji* (任昉述异记), but it is much simpler and less detailed than this account.

There is a mermaid in the reed pond, which transforms every five days. Sometimes it transforms into a beautiful and extraordinary woman, sometimes into a man, and the transformations are particularly numerous. The people of the county warn each other, so they dare not have harmful intentions, and the mermaid cannot plan against them. Later, it was killed by lightning, and this pond dried up. This passage is from the *Taiping Guangji* (太平广记).

In the commandery of Yuzhang, there was a place called Lu Song Encampment. According to the *Guangji* (Record of Unusual Things), a man named Luo Gensheng lived near this encampment, where he cultivated land and grew melons. He also built a shrine nearby. When the melons began to grow vines, one morning as he passed by the shrine, he suddenly saw a newly written board with black ink, which read: "This is the place where the deity roams. Do not stop here. Cultivate quickly and leave." Luo Gensheng bowed in gratitude and swore, saying: "I suspect that someone from the encampment may be using this fertile land to grow crops and might be pretending that the deity is driving me away. If this is indeed a divine command, I wish to have it written in red ink as a favor." The next morning, when he went to check, the board was still there, with all the black ink replaced by red. Luo Gensheng thanked the deity and left. (From *Guangji*, Volume 294) In the western part of the county, there is a Red City Mountain, which is about thirty li in circumference and has a peak that rises more than three hundred zhang. During the Taiyuan period of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, a foreigner named Bai Daoyou lived on this mountain. The mountain deity often sent wolves with strange shapes and eerie sounds to frighten him, but Bai Daoyou remained unperturbed. The deity then came to him and said: "Master, your virtue and power are great. I now offer this mountain to you, and I will seek another place to reside." Bai Daoyou asked: "Who are you? How long have you lived here? If you must leave, where will you go?" The deity replied: "I am the son of the king of the Xia Dynasty. I have lived here for over a thousand years. My maternal uncle resides at Hanzhi Mountain, and I will go there to stay temporarily. In the future, I plan to return to the Kuaiji Mountain Temple." Before leaving, the deity left a letter and gave him three boxes of incense. He also came personally to bid farewell, shaking hands with Bai Daoyou and expressing his regret. The deity then departed, passing through the air with the sound of drums and horns. (From *Guangji*, Volume 294)

The region of Hézhōu Lìyáng became submerged as a lake. First, there was a scholar who met an old woman, who treated him kindly. The scholar told the old woman, "The stone turtle's eyes on the gate of this county bleeded; this place will sink into a lake." The old woman then frequently visited to check on the situation. The gatekeeper asked the old woman, who fully informed him. The officials then marked the turtle's eyes with red. When the old woman saw this, she ran up the north mountain, and the city then sank. This story is from "Miscellaneous notes on various topics, volume ten." Traveling sixty li north from the mouth of the river, one reaches the southern stream of Teng Island. There is fresh water, clear enough to see the bottom, and there are crabs there. The crab's shell is as large as a bamboo hat, and its legs are three feet long. During the Yuanchi era of the Song Dynasty, a resident of Zhang'an killed a tiger and ate this crab, which was unusually delicious. That night, the tiger dreamt of a young woman speaking to it, saying, "You ate me; do you know you will soon be eaten?" The next day, the tiger went out and was eaten by the tiger. His family buried him, but the tiger dug up the coffin and ate him, leaving no remains. There are still large crabs in this water, and no one dares to touch them again.

Yuan Ke planted five-colored fragrant grasses, and five-colored moths gathered upon them. During the silkworm season, a woman came to raise silkworms and obtained one hundred and twenty cocoons, each as large as a jar. The woman and Yuan Ke both ascended to immortality. This account is from *Zhu Yi's Miscellaneous Notes on the Bedchamber*. In the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, the governor of Xuancheng, Feng Shao, suddenly transformed into a tiger and began to eat the people of his jurisdiction. The people called him "Governor Feng" and he left permanently. At that time, it was said, "Do not become Governor Feng; in life you do not govern the people, but in death you eat them." This story is recorded in *Maritime Records of Miscellaneous Events*. In the year of Huanglong during the Wu kingdom, in Haiyan, there lived a man named Lu Dongmei, whose wife was Zhu. Both had graceful manners, and the couple deeply cherished each other, never parting for even a step. They were known as "Shoulder-to-Shoulder People" by the people of their time. The couple believed that being inseparable like winged birds might not be as good. After his wife's death, Lu Dongmei refused to eat and sought to die, but his family mourned and buried them together. Within a year, a pair of willow trees grew from the same root and embraced each other. A pair of cranes often perched on the tree. Sun Quan, hearing of this, lamented and named the area "Shoulder-to-Shoulder Tomb" and "Double Willow." Later, their son Hong and his wife Zhang were also deeply affectionate, and the people of Wu called them "Little Shoulder-to-Shoulder People." This account is from *Miscellaneous Records*. --- I hope this translation meets your requirements. Let me know if you need any further adjustments or additional information.

Lu Ji, when young, was quite fond of hunting. During his time in Wu, a guest presented him with a swift dog named Huang'er. After Lu Ji became an official in Luoyang, he often took the dog with him. This dog was clever and intelligent, able to understand human speech. It had once traveled three hundred li on its own and returned home the next day. When Lu Ji was away in the capital for a long time and had no news from home, he jokingly asked the dog, "There is no letter from home, can you carry a letter to fetch news?" The dog happily wagged its tail and made a sound to respond. Lu Ji then wrote a letter, placed it in a bamboo tube, and tied it to the dog's neck. The dog set off along the post road, running swiftly towards Wu. When hungry, it would enter the grass to eat meat and satisfy itself. Whenever it encountered large bodies of water, it would lower its ears and wag its tail towards the people crossing, who would feel pity and allow it to board their boats. As soon as it neared the shore, the dog would jump onto the boat and continue on its way as fast as the wind. It arrived directly at Lu Ji's home, where it barked to indicate the presence of the letter. Lu Ji's family opened the bamboo tube, took out the letter, and read it. The dog then barked again, as if making a request. The family wrote a reply, placed it in the bamboo tube, and tied it to the dog's neck again. Once the dog had the reply, it ran back to Luoyang. While a human journey would take five months, the dog's round trip took only half a month. After the dog died, it was buried, and its remains were sent back to be buried at the south of Lu Ji's camp, two hundred steps from his home. The people at the camp called the grave "Huang'er's Tomb."

In Xunyang Chaisang County during the Yonghe era of the Jin Dynasty, there was a children's rhyme that called the city "Ping Shicheng," which was interpreted by the people as a sign of the destruction of stone, symbolizing the fall of power. Later, Huan Xuan usurped the throne, and the Jin Emperor became King Pinggu, while Emperor Gong became Duke Shi Yang, both of whom were relocated to this city. In the tenth year of the Yonghe era of Yao Xing's reign, the ground in the eastern boundary of Mount Hua burned, affecting an area over a hundred steps wide, with grass and trees scorched and withered, wells and valleys boiling and drying up, and all living things cooked, which was seen as a sign of the suffering of the people. In the fifth month of the first year of Guangxi in the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, the ground in the north of the state of Fanyang burned and could be used for cooking. By September, the Prince of Fanyang, Sima Xiao, had died. In November, Emperor Huai died while eating, and Emperor Huai ascended the throne. Sima Yue, the Prince of Donghai, killed the Chief Chancellor, Sima Yong, the Prince of Hejian, who then took control of the government, but soon died, leading to the Yongjia Rebellion. The Prince of Donghai was destroyed, and his successors were also annihilated. Shi Le burned Sima Yue's body, which was the fulfillment of these omens.

Huan Chong, serving as the governor of Jiangzhou, sent people to explore Mount Lu, hoping to witness supernatural phenomena. Having ascended to a high peak, they encountered a lake surrounded by mulberry trees; there was a large flock of white geese, and in the lake, there were damaged boats and red-scaled fish. The envoy was extremely thirsty and wanted to go drink water; the red-scaled fish displayed its fins towards him, and the envoy dared not drink. In the study where Governor Huan Huo of Jingzhou resided, he saw a person over ten feet tall, who said in a dream: "I am the deity of Long Mountain, and I came with no good intention; since you are steadfast and resolute, I will leave on my own!" At the end of the Yuanxing era of the Jin dynasty, a girl named Wan from the Chen family in Wei Prefecture, who lived in Chazhu, was sixteen years old. During a year of famine and plague, her parents died one after another, and she had only one brother who supported himself by working for hire. The girl was very beautiful, and neighbors, both commoners and officials, seeing her poverty and weakness, competed to offer her gold and silk. The girl maintained her integrity and never agreed to their offers. Later, during the rebellion led by Lu Xun, the rebels were about to assault her. The girl remained resolute and did not yield, and ultimately suffered harm.

In the fourth year of Yixi, Lu Xun, while in Guangzhou, secretly plotted rebellion and secretly sent someone to the Nankang temple to pray and petition. After offering sacrifices and beating drums, the messenger saw a single person wearing a military cap and red clothing, sitting in the middle of the banquet, who said, "If Lu Zhenglu rises in rebellion, he will be met with water here." In the sixth year of spring, Lu Xun then led his troops directly to Changsha; he sent Xu Daofu across the mountains to Nankang, where he prepared twelve ships, each with a warship tower over ten zhang tall. The ship preparations were just completed when it rained heavily for a day and night, causing the water level to rise by four zhang. Xu Daofu rode the waves downward, meeting Lu Xun at Bali, and when they reached the capital, Lu Xun was defeated in battle. Unaware that the gods would swiftly punish him, the great flood came, making him bring his own downfall. *Comprehensive Records* 295 In the fifth year of Yixi, Emperor Wu of Song campaigned against the Xianbei in the north and achieved a great victory, advancing to besiege Guangfu. The military leaders then sent an envoy to offer sacrifices and gifts to the Daiyue temple. There was a female witch named Qin from Fenggao, the widow of the Sù family, who could communicate with spirits and convey teachings, and her words were never false. The messenger set up a prayer and asked about the date of victory. Qin then declared the divine teaching, saying, "Divine grant of a heroic helper, the gods and demons are in awe, victory without battle; a small and insignificant enemy, not worth controlling. By the fifth day of the second month next year, they will be defeated." As predicted, the Three Qis were pacified. *Comprehensive Records* 283

During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, there was a man named Liu Dun who lived in Jiangling. Suddenly, a ghost appeared at his house. Liu Dun was poor and had no stove, so he used a wooden stick (汴枪) to cook rice. Just as the rice was about to be cooked, it disappeared. He searched for it among the grass near the fence but only found an empty stick. Liu Dun secretly bought a kind of plant (冶葛) known for its toxicity, cooked it to make porridge, and the ghost again stole it from the house. When he found the stick, he heard a sound of spitting, and from then on, the ghost was quiet and gone. *Imperial Reader* 990. Gan Luo, a descendant of Murong Wei (also known as Murong Wei), was the twelfth (or eleventh) ancestor of the Xianbei people. He wore golden and silver armor, rode a white horse with golden and silver saddle and reins, and fell from the sky. The Xianbei people revered him, and he was elected as their leader. *Book of Han* 129 and *Book of the Later Han* 126, *Imperial Reader* 356 and 695. In the fourth year of Fu Jian's reign, a tall person was seen, five zhang tall. He spoke to a man named Zhang Jing, saying, "Now there will be peace and prosperity." The magistrate of Xinping reported this to Fu Jian, who thought it was a false prophecy and summoned Zhang Jing, imprisoning him. That month, there was continuous rain in the Yellow River and Wei River, and the rivers overflowed, reaching the city of Pu Ban. A ferry supervisor named Kou Zou found a large shoe in the middle of the river, seven chi and three cun long, with a footprint matching the shoe. The fingerprints were more than one chi long, and the depth of the print was seven cun. *A Primer for Beginners* 19, *Imperial Reader* 377, citing Zu Chongzhi.

姚苌 after killing苻坚, fought against苻煮in the east of陇. One night,姚苌dreamed that苻坚's general, the heavenly messenger, led troops into姚苌's camp and stabbed姚苌with a spear, directly hitting his genital area.姚苌was startled awake and found his genital area swollen and painful. The next day, he died. (御览四百) During the reign of秦,周访was young and traveled upstream on the江with merchants. In the evening, they stopped at the宫亭temple. The companions asked each other, "Who can stay in the temple for the night?"周访had a bold and decisive nature, so he went up to the temple and stayed there, remaining calm all night. In the morning, he saw an old man with white hair in the temple and captured him. The old man transformed into a male duck.周访caught him and brought him back to the boat, intending to cook him, but the duck flew away. Later, nothing else happened. (珠林三十二) In the second year of永康(引作承康), according to广记, a ghost called out in the都卫, saying, "Brothers killing each other, the people dying, the two吕families perishing." The patrol officer followed the sound and looked, but saw nothing. That year,吕光died, and his son绍ascended the throne for five days.绍's half-brother篡took over and established himself as the ruler. The above is also found in广记三百二十一. The following year,吕光's younger brother, the车骑大将军常山公, repeatedly achieved military victories and was suspected of谋反. He led his troops to attack赞. They killed and drank to excess, wandering without restraint. The next year, he was killed by his younger brother起while drunk.起elevated his brother隆as the leader.姚兴, troubled by the people, sent his uncle, the征西general陇西prince硕德, to attack them.隆's army was defeated and scattered, and soon they were conquered by姚氏. (占经一百十三) 王子项was in荆州in the second year of永光. The柏tree in his position broke, and the beams and rafters were all wet. The juice dripped to the ground, and the next year he was executed. (占经一百十四)

Zhang Gui, courtesy name Shiyan, was appointed as the envoy with imperial authority and the Protector of the Qiang people and governor of Liangzhou. A guest divined and said, "Prosperity will pass down to his descendants, and the Western Regions will remain prosperous. When the capitals of Guanzhong and Luoyang fall, the land of Liang will remain intact. He served in office for thirteen years, passing the state through three generations and eight rulers for a total of sixteen years." (From *Yulian* 6083) Zhang Jun fell ill and dreamed of traveling and sightseeing, not recognizing the place. Sweet springs gushed out, and a dark-colored turtle spoke to Zhang Jun with an open mouth, saying, "In nine more days, there will be good news." He suddenly awoke, recorded it in his own writings, sealed it in a tube, and no one knew about it. Due to illness, he died nine days later. (From *Yulian* 400) After Zhang Jun's death, his son Zhonghua succeeded him. Liu Yao sent General Wang Zhuo to capture Wu Yu Shi Shi and besieged Bao Han together with others. Zhonghua sent Song Ji to lead the troops against them. They crossed the Yellow River and camped in Jin Cheng, preparing for a decisive battle. Then, a black rainbow descended into the camp. A few days later, Song Ji died of illness. (From *Yulian* 14) When Emperor Song Taizu was in humble circumstances, he once traveled to Kuaiji and passed by Kong Jing's house. During the day, he lay down, and a divine being with unusual clothing said to him, "Rise, for the emperor is at the door." The being then disappeared. Kong Jing hurriedly came out and met the future emperor, inviting him in and forming a friendship, exchanging gifts. At the moment of parting, Kong Jing took the emperor's hand and said, "You will surely become very powerful in the future. I wish to entrust my son to you." The emperor agreed. After establishing the capital city, Kong Jing was promoted from the position of Shan Yin County Magistrate to the position of Kuaiji Internal Historian. (From *Yulian* 128)

甄法崇在永初年间担任江陵县令，为官严明。当时，南平的僇士担任江安县令，因故被罢免，最终在那一年去世。甄法崇在厅堂上，忽然看见一个人从门外进来，说道：“僇江安通法崇。”甄法崇知道僇士已经去世，便问道：“你为何如此消瘦？”那人回答说：“我生前行为不善，恶行多于善行，如今被罚做苦役，处境极为困苦。”（见《御览》卷三百七十八） 在宋文帝时期，天水的梁清家住在北京的新亭，腊日那天准备祭祀，他派婢女到厨房做饭，忽然感到空中有物，手持棍棒打婢女，婢女跑去告诉梁清。梁清便前往查看，只见碗碟自行摆好饭菜和羹汤，摆放在案桌上，还能听到咀嚼的声音。梁清问道：“为何不现身？”这时，他看见一个人戴着平顶头巾，穿着黑色皮裤和夹衣，说道：“我是京兆人，死后流落于此，听说你尊重学者，所以前来相随。”梁清便在地上铺席，与他一同坐下，摆上菜肴和酒。鬼魂说：“你有祭祀之事。”梁清计划出任某郡太守，先去询问鬼魂，鬼魂说：“你所谋划的必定会实现，某月某日你就会被任命。”果然如此。鬼魂又说：“那郡很闲适，我愿意与你相伴。”梁清表示同意。后来，梁清停船在石头城，等待五天，鬼魂仍未出现。于是，梁清引路前往彭城，鬼魂才现身。两人在那郡共处数年，梁清返回京城时，鬼魂也随他一同返回。（见《广记》卷三百二十三）

The Carriage and Cavalry General, Prince Liu Yixuan of Nanqiao, was stationed in Jingzhou. In his household, there was a servant named Cai Tie, who was skilled in divination and could accurately predict outcomes. He occasionally had profound insights and investigated matters with divine precision. Once, the prince was in his inner study and saw a white rat climbing up the beam. He ordered his attendants to shoot it and placed it in a box. At that time, six attendants were all driven into a small room behind the study. The prince then separately called someone to summon Cai Tie. When Cai Tie arrived, he could accurately predict what was inside the box. The prince said to him, "If you hit it, you will receive a generous reward; if you miss, you will face severe punishment." Cai Tie completed his divination and smiled, saying, "I already know everything." The prince said, "Describe it." Cai Tie described, "A white rat with a bright back, shot from the bow, severed its left thigh. The rat is pregnant with five offspring, three males and two females. If you do not believe me, cut open its belly to see." The prince then ordered the rat's belly to be cut open, and everything was as Cai Tie had said. He immediately rewarded him with ten thousand coins.

During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (424–453 CE), a man named Huang Miao from Pinggu, Nan'gang, was a state official who took leave and violated the deadline. Just before traveling upstream, he passed through Gongting Lake and made a wish at the temple, hoping to avoid punishment and also wanting to return home. If both wishes were fulfilled, he promised to offer pig and wine. When Huang Miao arrived at the state, all his wishes were fulfilled, and he returned home. With meager resources, he did not pass by the temple and traveled to the capital's boundary, where he docked for the night with companions. Midway through the night, the boat suddenly descended from the water as swiftly as the wind, at midnight during the fourth watch. Huang Miao arrived at Gongting and only then did he awaken. He saw three men on the boat, all wearing black robes, holding ropes to bind him. At night, they ascended the temple steps. There, he saw a deity, about forty years old, wearing a yellow and white brocade robe, with a pearl hanging from the beam, as large as a bullet, its light illuminating the room. One person outside the door said, "Huang Miao from Pinggu, who vowed to offer pig and wine but fled home, is now captured." The deity ordered a three-year exile and took thirty people.

A Man Transformed into a Beast Officials were sent to escort the young man into the deep mountains and forests, where his waist was tied to a tree, and he was fed with raw meat every day. The young man was suddenly filled with worry and grief, and he only felt cold, heat, and sores on his body. His whole body grew spotted fur. After ten days, his body was covered in fur, and his claws and teeth grew, and he desired to attack and bite. The officials untied him and followed his movements. After three years, they had taken a total of twenty-nine people. Next, a woman from Xingan should be taken, but this woman was from a noble family and initially did not go out. Later, she happened to go out with her sisters-in-law from the back door to visit relatives, and she was last in line, so they took her. Because this woman was hard to obtain, it took five years, and the number of people was finally complete. The officials sent him to the temple, where the god ordered them to release him. They fed him with salted rice, and his body hair gradually fell off. All his hair grew out, his claws and teeth fell off, and new ones grew. After fifteen days, he returned to human form, and his thoughts returned to normal. They sent him out on the main road. The county magistrate called the young man to prepare a detailed report, reviewing the people taken before and after, and asked all their families, and they all matched. His thigh was injured by a halberd, and the scar is still there. The young man returned home after eight years and died of a seasonal illness. Recorded in the *Guangji* (296).

Nankang County, District Official Yingmin Qu Jingzhi, in the first year of the Yongjia era of the Song Dynasty (424 AD), traveled upstream with his son in a boat from the county into a small, remote, dangerous, and inaccessible stream, a place where human footprints have never been. They stopped for the night at the shore and stayed in a shelter. Qu Jingzhi suddenly died from a severe illness. His son lit a fire to keep watch over the body. Suddenly, he heard a distant weeping sound calling "Uncle." The filial son was startled and suspicious. In a moment, the weeping sound became audible. The apparition appeared like a grown person, with hair covering the feet and face, and no visible orifices. It called out the son's name to comfort him. The son was afraid and used all the firewood to light a fire. The apparition said, "I came to comfort you. What should you fear? Do you need a fire?" The apparition sat beside the dead person's head and cried. The son peeked at it secretly in the firelight. He saw the apparition cover the dead person's face with its face. In a moment, the dead person's face peeled off the skin and exposed the bones. The son was afraid and wanted to strike it but had no weapons. In a moment, the father's body showed only white bones. Continuously, the skin and bones were completely gone. In the end, it was unknown what kind of ghost or spirit this was.

In the early years of the Yuanchao era of the Southern Song dynasty, General Wang Zhongde, the General of the North, was stationed in Pengcheng. His attendants went hunting and encountered a crane with two chicks. They captured all of them and returned, presenting them to the General, who ordered them to be kept. The smaller chick had its mouth split by humans and thus could not eat or drink. The larger chick would take grains and feed it, and when drinking, it would take water and give it to the smaller one, first making sure it was full, never failing to do so. The General greatly loved them and ordered them to be cared for carefully. The larger chick's feathers developed first, and it frequently soared into the sky; the smaller one was still unable to fly, but the larger one never left first, staying behind to feed it. It also leaped in the courtyard, teaching it to fly. After more than sixty days, the smaller one could now fly, and then they flew away together. *From the *Tongdian*, Volume 916.* In Qingzhou, there was a person named Liu in the early Yuanchao era. He shot a deer, cut open its belly, and stuffed it with grass. The deer suddenly stood up and quickly walked forward. Liu found it strange and pulled out the grass. Soon, the deer returned to lie down, and this happened three times. Liu secretly recorded this phenomenon to study its nature, and many wounds healed as a result. *From the *Guangji*, Volume 443.*

In the early years of the Yungang period (424–453 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty, a man surnamed Wang from Fuyang made a crab trap in a small, remote stream. In the morning, he went to check it and saw a piece of wood about two feet long in the trap, which had broken open, and all the crabs had come out. He then repaired the trap and took the wood to the shore. The next morning, he went to check it and saw the wood again in the trap, broken as before. Wang repaired the trap and took the wood out again. The next morning, he went to check it, and what he saw was the same as before. Wang suspected that this piece of wood was strange or supernatural. He then took it and put it in a crab cage, tying it to his shoulder pole and went home, saying he would chop it up and burn it when he got home. Before he reached home, three miles away, he heard something moving inside the cage. He turned around and saw that the piece of wood had turned into something with a human face, a monkey body, one hand, and one foot. The creature spoke to Wang, saying, "I have a strong liking for crabs. These days I have indeed been entering the water to break your crab trap and eating the crabs. I have already wronged you. I hope you will forgive me and release me from the cage. I am a mountain god. I will help you, and your trap will catch many crabs." Wang replied, "You have wronged people. You have committed many crimes. You should die." The creature repeatedly begged to be released, but Wang turned his head and did not respond. The creature said, "What is your surname and name? I want to know." It kept asking repeatedly, but Wang did not answer. As he got closer to home, the creature said, "Since you won't release me and won't tell me your name, what can I do? I will just die." Wang reached home and burned the creature with fire. Afterward, there was no more strange occurrence. The local people called it a mountain demon, and the *Guangji* says that if it knows a person's name, it can harm them. That is why it kept asking Wang, wanting to harm him and save itself. *Zhu Lin* (Volume 31), *Guang Ji* (Volume 323).

Guo Zhongchan's residence was located to the south of the Bijia Temple in Jingling. During the Yuanjia period of the Liu Song dynasty, he built a house with bamboo window frames, which gradually grew into a dense, forest-like structure, several zhang tall, and Guo Zhongchan interpreted this as a sign of good fortune. However, during the Xiaojian period, he was executed. According to the *Taiping Guangji* (360), this occurred during the Yuanjia period, while the *Yulin Guangji* (375) mentions it as occurring during the Yuanwei period. In Wu County, Zhongdu District, there was a man named Shi Xuandu whose family had a yellow dog that gave birth to a white male puppy. The mother dog treated her white puppy differently from her other puppies, feeding it with her mouth. As the puppy grew into a large dog, it would often go hunting and not return, prompting the mother dog to wait outside the door for its return. Shi Xuandu had been suffering from a chronic cough for a long time and was becoming increasingly ill. A doctor prescribed a decoction that required the lung of a white dog. Despite searching the market for a white dog, they could not find one, so they had to kill the white puppy they had raised to provide the lung for the decoction. The mother dog, upon seeing her son's death, leaped and howled, falling to the ground and then rising again, continuing this behavior for several days without rest. The family cooked the puppy's meat and shared it with guests, throwing the bones on the ground, which the mother dog would retrieve and place in a den. After eating, they moved the bones to the backyard under a large mulberry tree, dug a hole, and buried them. The mother dog would then howl towards the tree daily, continuing this behavior for over a month before stopping. As Shi Xuandu's condition worsened, he repeatedly said before his death, "The decoction did not cure my illness, and I regret having killed this dog." His brother, Faxuandu, vowed never to eat dog meat again. (According to the *Yulin Guangji* 437)

An Guo Li Daoyu, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (424-453 CE), had a dog lying in the road. Li Daoyu tripped it, and the dog said, "You are going to die, why do you step on me?" Li Daoyu died shortly after. (Recorded in the *Guangji* Volume 438) Yu Jisui was a man of strong character and extraordinary physical strength. During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, he fell ill and lay down during the day. A white vapor like clouds appeared in his room, about five feet high, and soon transformed into a rooster, flying and landing on another bed. Yu Jisui struck it, and it made a sound upon being struck, disappearing instantly, with blood flowing profusely on the ground. He then heard the weeping sound of a barbarian woman, who called out "my child" and came directly to the place of the blood. Yu Jisui struck again, and a creature resembling a monkey appeared, running out of the house, glaring at Yu Jisui with anger, and suddenly disappeared. By evening, two blue-clad children entered through the door, shouting, "Yu Jisui has killed the official!" Soon after, a hundred people, some wearing black, some wearing red, and some wearing houseclothes, all shouted together, "Yu Jisui has killed the official!" Yu Jisui swung his knife and shouted loudly, and all the ghosts fled and disappeared. He suddenly entered a temple; his son suddenly lost sight of his father and went to the temple, where he saw a ghost following behind his father. He used a leather bag to collect the ghost's breath, and his father died after several days. (Recorded in the *Guangji* Volume 325)

A man from Nankang named Deng Deming once studied at Yuzhang under Lei Cizong. Lei's home was located outside the eastern suburbs, about half a li from the tomb of Shi Yuzhang. In the fourteenth year of the Yuanjia reign, Deng Deming, along with his fellow students, took a leisurely walk under the moon. Suddenly, they heard the sounds of music and recitation. That very night, Deng Deming informed Lei, who went out to listen and said, "This place is still far from people; it must be the ghosts or spirits." They then went together to investigate, arriving at a distance from Shi's tomb. They only heard the sounds of stringed instruments, women singing, recitations, and poetry being chanted from beneath the mound. All of them marveled at the unusual occurrence. (Yuelan, Volume 559) Thin Shaozhi once served as a military advisor to Zang Zhi. In the twenty-fourth year of the Yuanjia reign, he temporarily resided in a guesthouse on the west side of the Eastern Capital. He lived next to Zu Fa Kai. Zu's mother Liu had been ill for many days and passed away on the first day of the fifth month of the twenty-second year of the Yuanjia reign at midnight. On the second day, Shaozhi saw a group of mice, some as large as pigs, with fresh and lustrous fur in various colors, some solid, some mixed, some wearing flat-topped hats, others with dragon heads, in various sizes, for many days and nights. By the nineteenth day at dusk, there was a white mouse about two feet long on the eaves of the inner house, which entered under the wall. Where it entered, a fire started. When they poured water over it, the fire did not go out but extinguished itself after a long time. That night, they saw a tall and strong man with a red body, whose light was like fire, coming out from the burned wall, passing through the bed, and then emerging outside the wall. Even though there was a wall between them, the light was so bright and clear that they did not feel any barrier. In the fourth watch, four more people appeared, some speaking words of support to Shaozhi, others glaring and sticking out their tongues, and the sentence ends abruptly.

Until dawn. The next night, the house was set on fire again, and there were two men about nine feet tall, riding horses and carrying bows and arrows, accompanied by dozens of people, who called themselves generals. Shao asked, "Where are you going?" They replied, "We were sent to visit sick people in the east and return." On the twenty-first day, the group returned. There was a white dog in the family, which often disappeared due to ghosts and returned by dawn. That night, try to tie it up. Soon, a woman came and said, "Do not tie this dog, I wish to ask for it." The response was, "Then give it to you." Throwing the rope, he still dared not untie it, and suddenly walked out. The dog then groaned and was nearly dead, unable to move for days. A person in a brocade robe, holding a bow and arrows, aimed directly at. Shao said, "You are an evil spirit, dare to frighten people? I am not afraid of you, if you do not leave quickly, I will have the great road gods capture and punish you." The ghost drew the bowstring and shot the arrow, then rode away on horseback. This is recorded in the "Yan Tie Lu" (Yan's Record), entry 325.

In the town of Gao Yao in Jiaxing County, Zhu Xiuzhi had a younger brother named Zhu Yuan. In the 25th year of the Yuanchang era (448 AD), on a clear morning in the tenth month, the brothers were sitting together at home. Suddenly, a dog came and approached Zhu Xiuzhi, examining both brothers thoroughly before laughing. It then shook its head and began to sing: "I cannot sing, but listen to me sing of the plum blossoms. This year is still fine, but what will you do next year?" The family was terrified and, in a fit of panic, killed the dog and posted its head on the roadside. By the end of the year, when the plum blossoms were in bloom, the brothers fought, and the younger brother wounded the elder with a spear. The authorities arrested them, and they were imprisoned for a whole year before being released. In the summer, the entire family fell ill, and the mother and brothers all died. This account is also recorded in the *Taiping Yuangui* (卷九百七十、卷八百八十五、卷九百五) and the *Lüji* (类聚八十六), though the version in Ren Fang's *Shu Yi Ji* is somewhat briefer.

A Man from Gao Ping In the twenty-fifth year of the Yuanjia era, Cao Zongzhi of Gao Ping was staying in Pengcheng. He slept through the night without waking, and in the morning, he appeared to have died. By the late afternoon, his breath returned to normal, and he recounted what he had seen. He saw a man wearing a single robe and a headband, holding a tablet, who called himself a messenger of the Prince of the Northern Sea and said that the prince wished to summon him. Cao Zongzhi followed the messenger to the front of the palace, where the central courtyard was filled with a light cloud that hovered several dozen feet above the ground, casting flowing shadows and pausing. Between the curtains, there was a purple smoke that drifted and fluttered. The wind carried the smoke close to the people, and its fragrance was extraordinary. The messenger said, "You wait at the steps while I go inside to report." A short while later, he returned with a message: "Thank you, Mr. Cao. Your service is commendable, and I have long admired you. I now wish to humbly request that you serve as an assistant in my government. How old are you this year, and have you ever held a high-ranking official position?" Cao Zongzhi replied, "I have always been of weak ability and am ashamed before your majesty's grace. I am thirty-one years old and have never held a high-ranking official position." The messenger then said, "Although your age is young, you have previously had good karma and should receive a prominent and important position. You should pass through the ranks of high-ranking officials. For now, you may return home, and we will discuss further later." Soon, the messenger escorted him out the door, and Cao Zongzhi woke up dazedly. Later, Cao Zongzhi served in Guangzhou. At the age of forty-seven, the following year, he lost his position and returned to Guangzhou, where he fell ill and died. This account is recorded in the *Guangji* (377).

During the Song dynasty, Hu Bi Zhi from Yuzhang served as the County Magistrate of Wuchang. In the 26th year of the Yuanjia era (448 AD), when he entered his office, there were ghostly phenomena at midnight under the moonlight. The windows were slightly open, and someone stood leaning against the door, appearing like a child. When the door closed, one could hear the sound of footsteps like wooden clogs, but when looked at, nothing was seen. This happened many times. In the third month of the 28th year (451 AD), the entire family contracted a seasonal illness. There were voices in the air throwing tiles or dry earth. In summer, all the sick people were affected, and the intensity of the voices and throwing became even stronger. He then invited a Daoist to perform a ritual, and the entire night was spent reciting scriptures. They came in even greater numbers like rain, but they did not affect the Daoist or the scriptures. In autumn and winter, there were gradually more sounds, and tiles and stones were thrown at people, but they were dark and not very painful. Bi Zhi had an old dog that liked to bark at ghosts, and it barked loudly at the ghosts. Bi Zhi invited a priest to perform a ritual and used talismans to drive them away. They gradually ceased. In the 29th year (452 AD), the ghosts returned, more fierce than before. The following year, the county magistrate's office caught fire four times. They were in a hurry to pour water on the fire, and they all died of their illnesses. The case has a misspelled character, and the ghosts often made sounds like dogs. The family often called them "chī liàn." Later, they suddenly spoke, and their speech sounded like a cow. At three o'clock in the morning, they knocked on the door. Bi Zhi asked, "Who is it?" The answer was, "Cheng Shaoling." He lit a fire to look, but saw nothing.

After several days, at the second watch of the night, someone again knocked on the door and clapped, and then cursed. The spirit replied, "Do not curse me. I am a good spirit, not the previous or subsequent ones. Taoyushi has sent me to report to you." Bi Zhi said, "I do not know Taoyushi." The spirit said, "Taoyuanjun used to interact with him." Bi Zhi said, "I served in Hengyang with him when we were in the capital, and I never served as a censor." The spirit said, "Taoyuanjun is in a blessed place, serving as a celestial censor. The previous and subsequent intrusions were by Shen Gong. This office was originally Shen's residence. Because he came to see the house, he casually threw some tricks. You suddenly pushed back too much, even to the point of cursing. He ordered his maidservants to be disrespectful to you, and also ordered the Master of the Wine to submit a complaint against you. The matter reached the Heavenly Court. Shen now reports to the Heavenly Court: 'You are a disciple who has taken the Three Refuges. Why not seek blessings from the Buddha, instead you have the Master of the Wine to submit a complaint?' From now on, I only wish for you to devote yourself to the law, do not engage in evil, and the spirits will torment you." At that moment, there was doubt about the word "not." Bi Zhi asked the nuns to read scriptures, and then he fasted. After one night, he heard the censor report to the white tricolor, "The Chancellor saw that Shen Gong was very distressed. As he said, you were quite disrespectful. If you can sincerely repent and practice the scriptures and precepts, then all evil will be eliminated. Out of our former affection, I am telling you this." (Jewel Forest, Volume 46)

During the day, Suo Wanjing of Dunhuang was sitting in the hall. A servant in the eastern study room suddenly saw a man wearing a headband, leading a gray horse, and entering directly through the door. The man carried an object that looked like a black leather pouch, which he placed at the base of the wall, then led the horse out. The pouch began to rotate on its own, entering the study room, climbing up the bedpost, and stopping in front of Suo Wanjing's knees. The skin of the pouch then opened in four directions, revealing that it was filled with eyes all around, which moved and blinked in a very repulsive manner. After a long while, the pouch closed again and unfolded, continuing to rotate as it descended from the bed and moved westward. Suo Wanjing ordered the servant to chase the apparition to the east end of the hall, where it disappeared. Fearing the apparition, Suo Wanjing fell ill and died. This story is recorded in entry 325 of the *Yingyai Shenglan* (A Record of the Strange).

De Guo Xiu resided in Haiting, during the twenty-ninth year of the Yuangjia period of the Song dynasty, he was seventy-three years old and fell ill, staying confined to his main house. To the north, there was a large jujube tree, about four zhang tall. A young maid opened the door and swept the floor in the morning, and saw a person on the jujube tree. The person was tall and strong, with a dark complexion, wearing a morning cap, black leather trousers and a short jacket, holding a bow and arrow, standing upright facing south. The family came out to see. De Guo Xiu, leaning on a cane, looked at the person. The person said to De Guo Xiu, "I have come to summon you; you should prepare quickly." After sunrise, the person was no longer seen. This continued for fifty-three days. After De Guo Xiu's death, it stopped. As above. Tao Jizhi, at the end of the Yuangjia period, served as the magistrate of Moling. He killed a bandit, one of whom was a famous musician. Not a bandit, but Tao forced him to kill him. On the verge of death, he said, "I really did not rob; I was wrongfully killed. If I see a ghost, I will certainly appeal for justice." After a short time, the bandit, as recorded in the *Yiwen Leiju* volume 323, *Tongdian* volume 406, and *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* volume 23, came to him in a dream and said, "In the past, I was wrongfully killed and appealed to heaven for justice; now, I am here to take you." He jumped into Tao's mouth and fell into his stomach. After a short while, he came out again and said, "Now, we are only after Tao of Moling; there is no need for him. Let's discuss Wang of Danyang instead!" After speaking, he disappeared. Tao died shortly after. Wang of Danyang indeed died.

In Huangzhou, there is a Yellow Father Ghost that appears and causes harm. It wears yellow clothing and a yellow headband. When it reaches a household, it opens its mouth and laughs, inevitably bringing on plague or epidemic. Its height is variable, changing according to the height of the fence or wall. It has been appearing for over ten years, and the local people fear it. In Luxiang, there was a household servant named Cai Wei, who was young and beautiful. During the reign of Emperor Xiaojian of Song, suddenly there was a person who called himself the mountain spirit. He was naked and about three meters tall, with yellow arms and head, and a clean and neat appearance. His speech was standard and correct. The local people call him the Yellow Father Ghost, and he came to communicate with this servant. The servant said that her feelings were like those of a person, and the ghost frequently came to her. The ghost often hid his body, sometimes revealing his form. His form changed unpredictably, sometimes large and sometimes small. Sometimes he appeared like smoke, sometimes like stone, sometimes like a child, sometimes like a woman, and sometimes like a bird or animal. His footprints were like those of a person, about 60 centimeters long, and sometimes like a goose, with footprints as large as a plate. He opened and closed doors and windows as if by magic, and played and laughed with the servant like a person. This story is recorded in the *Guangji*, entry 325.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (453-456 AD), Fei Qingbo, who served as the state governor, took a temporary leave and returned home. Upon arriving, he suddenly saw three horsemen, all wearing red headgear, come together and say, "The official summons you." Fei Qingbo replied, "I have just returned from my leave; how could I be summoned? And you usually wear black headgear; why are you all wearing red now?" The horsemen answered, "This is not an official from this realm." Fei Qingbo then realized they were not human and knelt down, pleading with the three horsemen to speak in unison. They agreed to arrange a delay, saying, "We will return in four days. Prepare a little wine and food for us, and be careful not to reveal this." As promised, they returned on the fourth day and said, "We have already done what we could." Fei Qingbo was overjoyed and thanked them, personally setting up wine and food. He saw the ghost eat and drink just like a living person; before leaving, they said, "We pity you, so we ask you to keep this secret." Fei Qingbo’s wife, who was jealous and suspicious, told him, "This must be a trick by some evil spirit." Fei Qingbo, having no choice, told her the whole story. Soon, the three horsemen reappeared, beating him with whips until he bled, standing angrily before him and saying, "How could you deceive us?" After saying this, they disappeared. Fei Qingbo then fell ill suddenly and died before dawn. This story is recorded in *Guangji* (Widely Recorded), entry 326.

A Man from Yen-chuan During the reign of Emperor Hsiao-chien of the Song Dynasty, a man named Yu from Yen-chuan fell ill and died. His heart was still warm, and he had not been buried after spending one night. Suddenly, he spoke and recounted: Upon his initial death, two black-clothed individuals came to take him, binding and leading him forward. They showed him a large city with a high and steep gatehouse, heavily defended. They brought him into the hall, where many others had also entered. In the hall, a noble person sat facing south, attended by hundreds of servants, who called him Lord. Lord took a brush and asked those who had arrived, and when it came to Yu, he said, "This person's fate has not yet run out." He hurriedly sent him away. One person came up the stairs to lead Yu out, and at the city gate, the official sent someone to escort him. The gate official said, "Must report back before leaving." Outside the gate, a girl, about fifteen or sixteen years old, with a beautiful and composed appearance, said, "Mr. Yu is fortunate to return, but has been delayed like this. It is the gate official seeking something." Yu replied, "Just now was recorded, came lightly without anything to offer." The girl removed the bracelet from her left arm and threw it to Yu, saying, "Along with this, give it to him." Yu asked the girl her surname, and she said, "My surname is Zhang, and my home is in Mao-zhu. Last night I died in a disaster." Yu said, "I left behind five thousand coins when I died, intended to buy materials. If I am reborn, I will send this money as a repayment." The girl said, "Cannot bear to see you alone in distress. This is my personal property, no need to return it to my family." Yu finally did not report back, and the official sent someone else to send him away. Yu said goodbye to the girl, who sighed deeply and wept. Yu then suddenly woke up and went to Mao-zhu to search, indeed finding a newly deceased young woman from the Zhang family. (From the *Guangji*, volume 383)

Wang Yao died of illness in the third year of Da Ming, during his time in the capital. After Wang Yao's death, a ghost appeared in his household. It was slender and black, wearing a short apron-like garment, and it would often come to the house. Sometimes it sang or howled, and sometimes it imitated human speech, frequently throwing feces into the food. It also caused disturbances at the neighboring Yu household, behaving in the same way as it had in Wang's household. The Yu family told the ghost, "Throwing earth or stones at me is not what I fear, but if you throw coins at me, that would truly trouble me." The ghost then threw dozens of new coins directly at the Yu family's forehead. The Yu family replied, "New coins cannot hurt me, but I only fear old coins!" The ghost then threw old coins at them, doing so six or seven times in total, and collected over a hundred coins. In the sixth year of Da Ming, Bi Zhongbao, a man from Dongping who lived in Pengcheng, had a gray horse that was very fast. He often rode it in and out, treasuring it dearly. One night, Bi Zhongbao dreamed that his deceased elder brother Zhongqing appeared to him and said, "I have military service and am in a dangerous situation, and I have no fast horse. You can give me the gray horse." Bi Zhongbao agreed. After waking up, he called his fellow guest to tell him about the dream. Just then, he heard the sound of the horse falling and sent someone to check on it. The horse was barely breathing, looking as if it had been poisoned. Bi Zhongbao knew the reason in his heart and tried to treat it. The horse died in the morning, and Bi Zhongbao returned to lie down as if to sleep. He then heard Zhongqing say, "Just now I asked for a horse. You treated it with great care, and I will not regret it. Now I return it to you. I will look for another." By dawn, the horse had recovered and was eating as usual.

During the eighth year of the Daming era, the Grand General of the Cavalry from Hedong, Liu Yuanjing, served under a young emperor who had recently ascended the throne. Yuanjing returned home by carriage, and someone in the courtyard washed the carriage pole, drying it in the sun. Suddenly, a gust of wind entered through the central gate, rushing directly towards the carriage. The following year, his entire family was executed. *Jiyun* 808. During the Daming era, the county magistrate of Dunqiu, Liu Shun, after drinking heavily, woke up in the morning to find a clot of blood on the bed, shaped like an inverted bowl. Being a man of martial prowess, he was not surprised. He then ordered the preparation of a paste, personally cutting the blood and mixing it with the paste to eat. He discarded the remainder. After about ten years, in the second year of the Yuanwei era, he was harmed by Wang Daolong. *Jiyun* 808, *Guangji* 360. The Wu Kang Xu family, during the Taoyuan period (likely a misprint for "Daming"), suffered from malaria that was continuously treated but not cured. Someone advised them, "Make several balls of rice and throw them out the front door, call out the names of the deceased and say, 'To cure my malaria for me, I give this ball to you.' Throw it away and return directly, do not look back." The sick person followed the advice and called out the name of the former Jin dynasty's General of the Cavalry, Shen Chong. Soon, a person riding a horse with attendants arrived, asking, "Who are you, and why do you dare to name officials?" They tied him up and took him away. The family searched for days, finally finding him under a thicket near the house, where the rope was still there. The malaria was finally cured. *Jiyun* 766.

Liu Daocon, the brother of Liu Deyuan, had a sudden appearance of dozens of white earthworms on the front steps of his study in the fifth month of the first year of the Jinghe era. These earthworms were entirely white, something people had never seen before, and they all had their mouths open and tongues out, which were a bright red color. In the eighth month of that year, Liu Daocon was executed along with Liu Deyuan. (Yuelan 947, Guangji 473) Liu Dengzhi lived in the capital during the reign of Emperor Ming of Liu Song. He was in charge of all the sacred temples and was greatly favored. His mother, Mrs. Xie, worshiped Buddhism. In the fifth year of the Taishi era, a mysterious object appeared in the form of smoke and mist, with its head hanging down and touching the ground in front of the office. The creature's head and neck resembled a large red bird, and it drank water in the courtyard. They were frightened but thought it was a benevolent spirit; they poured more water for it, and it drank over a hundred dou of water until the water ran out and then left. Two years after Mrs. Xie died, half a year later, the emperor died the following year. From then on, the family declined. (Yuelan 885, Guangji 360)

In the administration of Shu Commandery, there was a man named Hu Zi from Yuzhang. During the fourth year of the Taishi era of the Song Dynasty, suddenly in the air there appeared an ancient tomb, covered in moss and lime. It would often fly and land in front of his mother, sometimes five or three at once, causing the entire family to be terrified. However, it never hit anyone, and after ten days, it stopped. This incident is recorded in Chapter 767 of the *Taiping Guangji*. During the Taishi era of the Song Dynasty, there was a man named Zhang Yi who was flogged, and his wounds were painful and did not heal. Someone taught him to burn the bones of the dead into powder and apply it to the wounds. He hired a child from the same room to cook on a hill and take a skull, which he then burned and applied to the wound. That night, there was a fire inside the house that burned the child's hand, and something in the air pressed the child's head into the fire, scolding, "Why did you burn my head? Now I will repay you with this fire!" The child shouted, "Zhang Yi burned it!" The entity replied, "You did not give it to Zhang Yi, how could Zhang Yi have burned it?" It pressed the child's head for a long time, and all the hair fell out, the skin and flesh were charred, and then it left. Zhang Yi was greatly terrified; he sent the remaining bones back to their original place and offered wine and meat to them, and there were no more disasters or strange occurrences. This incident is recorded in Chapter 46 of the *Zhu Lin*.

Wang Wenming, magistrate of Jiang'an at the end of the Song dynasty, had a wife who had been long-suffering from illness. His daughter made porridge for her mother outside the house, but when it was almost ready, it turned into blood. She discarded it and made another, and it also turned into blood again. This happened twice. The *Zhu Lin* quotes this incident, and soon after, the mother passed away. After that, the children cried before the spirit tablet, and they suddenly saw their mother lying on the spirit tablet, looking as she did in life. The children cried out in emotion, and she disappeared suddenly. Wang Wenming had previously loved the maid who served his wife, as noted in the *Guangji*. She was pregnant and about to give birth. On the day of his wife's burial, he had the maid guard the house, while everyone else went to the burial site. When the funeral procession began, the wife's spirit appeared, entered the house, and struck the maid. After that, the daughters prepared food for their father and killed a chicken. After skinning and washing it, the chicken suddenly jumped up, with its head raised and a loud crow. Wang Wenming soon died, and the other sons died one after another. *Zhu Lin* 95, *Guangji* 325. Zhu Daozhen once served as the magistrate of Chanling, and Liu Kuo of Nanyang served as a military officer in Jingzhou. They often played chess with each other, day and night, and the chessboard was never idle for a moment. Zhu Daozhen died on the 26th day of the 6th month in the 3rd year of the Yuzhong era of the Song dynasty. By the 9th month, Liu Kuo sat in his study, and he suddenly saw a person who gave him a letter saying, "A letter from Zhu of Chanling." Liu Kuo opened the letter and saw that it was Zhu Daozhen's handwriting. It said, "I often think of playing chess together, but fate has kept us apart. Now there is a connection. I hope you can come soon." After finishing reading the letter, Liu Kuo lost faith in its existence and gradually fell ill, and soon died. *Yulin* 753.

Zhu Tai lived in Jiangling during the Yuanhuai era of the Song Dynasty. After he died of illness and was not yet buried, his ghost suddenly appeared in form and returned to sit beside his body, comforting and encouraging his mother. Everyone saw this. The ghost directed the arrangements for the funeral, insisting on frugality. He told his mother, "Our family is poor, and I have also passed away, permanently separated from caring for me. How can the funeral expenses be extravagant?" In the city of Chengdu, Shu Prefecture, there was a boy named Zhang Bao'er, who was about ten years old. He became a Taoist priest and possessed spiritual insight and foresight. At times, he would drink several liters of strong gray liquid, claiming it was to cleanse his intestines and treat diseases. The One-Horned person was from Jiang, Ba Prefecture. He was around several hundred years old, and the common people had forgotten his name. He had a single horn on top of his head, thus he was called the One-Horned. Sometimes he would suddenly leave after a long time, and at other times he would remain silent for weeks. When he spoke, his intentions and meanings were subtle, and all could not understand them. His residence was solely governed by virtue, and he also had many teachings. One day, he bid farewell to his family and entered the river in front of the house, transforming into a carp, with the horn still on his head. Later, he would occasionally return, his appearance as it was in life, and he would drink and feast with his descendants, leaving after a few days.

尹雄年九十，左鬓生角，长半寸。类聚十八御览三百七十三又三百八十三 At the age of ninety, Yin Xiong had horns growing from his left temple, each half an inch long. This account is found in volume 18 of the *Lei Ji* and volumes 373 and 383 of the *Yuelan*. 逢桃杖居江夏，病疾困笃，频上奉章。夜中有物若豕，赤色，从十余人，皆操绳，入门周床一匝而去。往问道士张玄冥，冥曰：“见者祟物伏罪，乌衣入宅里社检护耳，疾寻当除。”自是平复也。书钞八十七 Feng Taizhang, who lived in Jiangxia, was gravely ill and frequently submitted petitions. One night, a red, pig-like creature appeared, followed by over ten people holding ropes. It entered the house, circled the bed once, and then departed. Feng sought the advice of the Daoist Zhang Xueming, who said, "The one who saw it was a malevolent spirit that has confessed guilt. The black-clad one has entered the house to protect the neighborhood; the illness will soon be cured." From then on, Feng recovered. This account is found in volume 87 of the *Shu Chao*. 荀镶字叔玮，事母孝，好属文及道术，潜栖却粒。尝东游，已上四句类聚一引作寓居江陵憩江夏黄鹤楼上，望西南有物，飘然降自霄汉，俄顷已至，乃驾鹤之宾也。鹤止户侧，仙者就席，羽衣虹裳，宾主欢对。已而二字御览引有辞去，跨鹤腾空，眇然烟灭。类聚六十三又九十御览事类赋注十八 案亦见任昉记 Xun Xiang, courtesy name Shuwěi, was filial to his mother and enjoyed writing and Daoist practices. He lived in seclusion and abstained from grains. Once, while traveling east, he resided in Jingling and rested at the Yellow Crane Tower in Jiangxia. He saw something in the southwest, descending gracefully from the sky. Soon, it arrived, and it was a guest riding a crane. The crane stopped beside the door, and the immortal took a seat, wearing feathered robes and a rainbow-colored skirt. The guest and host happily conversed. After a while, the *Yuelan* records that the guest departed, riding the crane into the sky, disappearing into the mist. This account is found in volume 63 of the *Lei Ji*, volume 90 of the *Yuelan*, and volume 18 of the *Shi Leifu Zhan*. It is also recorded in the writings of Ren Fang.

Zhang Yun, a native of Xunyang, often visited and rested in an ancient city near his hometown. One day, he suddenly encountered an old man who approached him and asked, "What is the name of this city?" Zhang Yun replied, "I don't know; it's just the city of Nanjun!" The old man then disappeared without a trace. After Zhang Yun had risen through the ranks and become the Governor of Nanjun, he decided to name the city after the old man's words, commemorating the mysterious encounter. Qi Cheng, a man from Yuzhang known for his exceptional abilities, once went fishing on a boat. In a short while, his boat was filled with fish. Suddenly, a creature emerged from the water, with rough, black scales and a length of about thirty-three meters. The creature had no visible head or tail. The boat's crew was terrified, but Qi Cheng remained calm and unperturbed. After Zhuge Jingzhi's death, his spirit occasionally appeared in his former residence. Once, when Shen Sengrong returned home from buying wine, he found that his warming device was missing. The ghost said, "You don't have a warming device, how can you drink wine?" Shen Sengrong then saw a copper spear appear from the air. The ghost said, "If you just agree, I won't see you again; you must give it as a gift." Shen Sengrong agreed and burned the spear, and the ghost disappeared, leaving the spear behind on his own body. When Xuanhou Zuxin, also known as Huan, served as the governor of Yan State, he died in office, and Shen Sengrong succeeded him. Zhuge Jingzhi's spirit appeared before Shen Sengrong, and there was a woven belt decorated with precious ornaments on the bed. Xuanhou Zuxin said, "This belt is very beautiful. Could you give it to me?" Shen Sengrong replied, "Very well." Then Xuanhou Zuxin said, "If you just agree, I won't see you again; you must give it as a gift. You can burn it in front of me." Shen Sengrong burned the spear in front of him, and when he looked, the belt was already on Xuanhou Zuxin's waist.

A bamboo plant grew in the courtyard of Zhang Xunmengting in Bashu, with its joints resembling a single door. He consulted Zhi Fadu, who said, "It will bring sudden prosperity, but it will not last long." Indeed, as he had said, it happened. (*Taiping Guangji* 276) A servant of the Zhou family in Chenliu, named Xingjin, went into the mountains to fetch firewood and fell asleep from exhaustion. In her dream, she saw a woman who told her, "I am near your head, and there is a thorn in my eye. Please help me remove it, and I will reward you generously." Upon waking, she saw a rotten coffin with a hole in the top, and a skull fell to the ground. Grass was growing in the eye sockets, and she removed the grass, placing it back inside the coffin. She then sealed the hole with bricks and found a pair of gold finger rings at the location where the skull had fallen. (*Liu Tie* 23; *Taiping Guangji* 276; *Taiping Yulan* 479; *Shu Chao* 133) Chen Min, the governor of Jiangxia, promised a silver staff to the deity of the Gongting Temple. Later, he replaced it with a silver-coated iron staff. When he returned the staff to the temple, the temple's oracle declared, "Chen Min's crime is unforgivable." They then placed the staff in a lake, where it floated on the surface. Chen Min's boat encountered a strong wind and capsized. (*Shu Chao* 133)

Gu Yü had an affair with the woman Guo Ning. They went to the shrine to make an agreement for her to become his concubine, vowing that those with divided loyalties would die. Gu Yü then refused to marry her. After two years, he suddenly heard that Guo Ning had died suddenly. Gu Yü went out to look around, and someone came, who was Guo Ning. She bowed her hands and sighed, and Guo Ning told him, "On the way back from the northern garrison, I encountered robbers on the road. They drew their weapons and forced me. Fearing death, I complied, but I could not maintain my chastity. I was blamed by the shrine god, and suddenly suffered from heart pain, dying within one night." Gu Yü said, "Let's stay here for the night." Guo Ning replied, "Humans and ghosts have different paths. Don't trouble yourself." She then wept, and tears wet her clothes. Cui Ji from Qinghe lived temporarily in Qingzhou. The daughter of the Zhu family, whose beauty was unparalleled, deeply attracted Cui Ji, who sought her hand and made an agreement for her to become his concubine. Later, at the third watch of the night, he suddenly heard someone knocking at the door. Cui Ji put on his clothes and went out to greet her. The girl wept and sobbed, saying, "I suddenly fell ill and died, taking away joy and love forever. I cannot bear the grief." She took out two pieces of silk from her bosom and gave them to Cui Ji, saying, "I recently wove this silk and wanted to make you trousers and shirts, but did not get to sew them. Now I am giving them to you as a parting gift." Cui Ji gave her eight feet of brocade in return. She took the brocade and said, "From now on, we are separated!" After she finished speaking, she disappeared suddenly. By morning, he told his family. The girl's father said, "The girl suddenly fell ill last night and died at night." Cui Ji asked, "Did your family lose any silk or cloth?" The father replied, "This girl had two pieces of silk she had woven and stored in a box. When the girl died, her mother took out the silk to make a burial garment, and it disappeared when she looked away." Cui Ji then fully recounted the events.

### Translation Lü Qizhi lived in the southern part of the county, where there was a temple dedicated to the god of *chupu*. Lü Qizhi had a daughter named Seng, who suddenly woke up from a dream and said, "The god of *chupu* sent a maid to welcome me. I sat in a small, round canopy, and a grand feast was laid out. The serving platters were made of gold and silver, and the cups and bowls were made of multicolored jade. I ate with the god, and I woke up after one night." (Recorded in *Guangji* 294) Wang Zhaizong of Taiyuan died, and after his death, his spirit appeared to his mother Liu and his wife Han and spoke to them. He asked his mother for wine, took a cup, and offered it to her, saying, "Good wine!" He then spoke to his wife, saying, "I will be separated from you for three years!" When the mourning period ended, his wife fell ill and said, "The meaning of being buried together, which was difficult in ancient times, if fortunate, is as if we are still alive, is it not the greatest wish?" She then died without taking medicine. (Recorded in *Guangji* 318) Zhou Yi from Runan married the granddaughter of Liu Dan from Pei. Zhou Yi's younger brother, who was the county magistrate of Aixian in Yuzhang, fell ill on the road and, knowing he would not survive, left his family behind and went with his brother to the county seat. He died after one night. His wife arrived, stood by his body, and Zhou Yi raised his hand to bid her farewell. She combed his hair and then removed her hairpin. After the funeral, she stayed in the bedroom, and Zhou Yi climbed onto the bed and said to her, "Although we have been together for a short time, we cared for each other deeply. It is unfortunate to reach this point. My elder brother was unkind and separated me from my family. I was unable to take leave at the end, which is truly regrettable! I raised my hand to bid you farewell and removed your hairpin, intending to rise, but the crowd's presence prevented me." From then on, he appeared every night to sleep with her, just as he had in life. (Recorded in *Guangji* 322)

A minor official from Wuchang named Wu Gan obtained a floating stone. He took it and thought it should be placed by the bedside, and it transformed into a woman. She was beautiful, and Wu Gan became her husband. This story is found in the *Shu Chao* (Book Collection) entry 77, and it is also recorded in the current edition of Ren Fang's records. Dong Yi, a young man from Chenliu, had a neighbor's daughter named Liang Ying who was young and beautiful. Dong Yi was deeply enamored and offered her gifts of pepper and treasures. Liang Ying accepted the gifts but did not yield to his advances. Later, Dong Yi's neighbor Zheng Chong stayed at Dong Yi's place. During the second watch of the night, there was a knocking sound at the door. Zheng Chong lay and looked, and also recognized Liang Ying. He told Dong Yi, "Liang Ying has come." Dong Yi was startled and rushed out to greet her. He took her arm and led her into the house, and they lay down together. Liang Ying then asked to leave, but Dong Yi held her and would not let her go. They spent the night talking, and Dong Yi wanted to keep her. He said he would prepare a pig for her to eat. After the meal, she left. Dong Yi got up, closed the door, and set up a canopy. Liang Ying then transformed into a fox and ran away from the roof. This story is found in the *Yulu* (Imperial Readings) entry 912.

Ren Kaozhi, a civilian from Nankang, was cutting wood for a ship when he suddenly saw a monkey pregnant on a large tree. He then boiled wood to chase the monkey, which leapt and flew like a bird. The tree was isolated, and there were people below. The monkey knew it could not escape, so it held onto a branch with its left hand and caressed its belly with its right hand. Kao Zhi captured the monkey, shook it violently and killed it, then cut open its belly and found a baby, almost ready to be born. That night, he dreamed of a person calling himself a god, rebuking him for killing the monkey. Afterward, Kao Zhi fell ill for a month, initially acting mad, then gradually transforming into a tiger, with fur and claws growing, and his voice changing, and he eventually escaped into the mountains, never to be seen again. Ma Daoyou was a clerk in the imperial office, and in the first year of Yongming, while sitting in the office, he suddenly saw ghosts all around him, but others did not see them. Soon, two ghosts entered his ears, pushed out his soul, which fell onto his shoes, and he pointed at it, asking, "Do you see it?" The others did not see it, and asked what the soul looked like. Ma Daoyou said, "The soul looks like a frog, and they said there was no hope for survival, and the ghosts are still in my ears." His ears swelled, and he died the next day. A monk named Fali from Xianming Temple in Guangzhou, early in the morning, went to the toilet and met a ghost, looking like a Kunlun, with both eyes yellow, naked and without clothes. Fali, who was strong, tied it to a pillar and beat it with a stick, but it remained silent. He then tied it with an iron chain to see if it could transform and escape. As it grew dark, the ghost was no longer there.

《述异记》佚名 庐山上有康王谷，巅事类赋注引作北岭有一城，号为钊城。天每欲雨，辄闻山上鼓角笳箫之声，声渐至城，而风雨晦合，屯人以为常候。已上亦见御览十事类赋注三并无声渐二句传云，此周康王之城，康王爱奇好异，巡历名山，不远而至。城中每得古器、大鼎，及弓、弩、金之属，知非常人之所处也。而山有“康王”之号，城又以“钊”为称，斯言将有征。御览八十五 庐山上有三石梁，长数十丈，广不盈尺，俯眄杳然无底。咸康中，江州刺史庾亮，迎吴猛，猛将弟子煮山游观，因过此梁。见一老公，坐桂树下，以玉杯承甘露，与猛，猛遍与弟子。又进至一处，见崇台广厦，玉宇金房，琳琅焜耀，晖彩眩目，多珍宝玉器，不可识名。见数人与猛共言，若旧相识。设玉膏终日。珠林三十一御览四十一又六百六十三

昔有人发庐山采松，闻人语云：“此未可取。”此人寻声而上，见一异华，形甚可爱，其香非常，知是神异，因掇而服之，得寿三百岁也。珠林三十六 南康南野有东望山，营民三人上山顶，有湖清深，又有果林，周四里许，众果毕植，间无杂木，行列整齐，如人功也。甘子正熟，三人共食，致饱讫，怀二枚欲以示外人，便还。寻觅向迳，回旋半日，迷不能得；即闻空中语云：“速放双甘，乃听汝去。”怀甘者恐怖，放甘于地。转眄即见归迳，乃相与俱却返。御览九百六十六又四百九十初学记二十八类聚八十六事类赋注二十七

南康有神名曰“山都”，形如人，长二尺余，黑色、赤目、发黄被身，于深山树中作窠，窠形如坚鸟卵，广记引作形如卵而坚高三尺许，内甚泽，五色鲜明，二枚沓之，中央相连。土人云：“上者雄舍，下者雌室。”傍悉开口如规，体质虚轻，颇似木筒，中央以鸟毛为褥。此神能变化隐身，罕广记引作卒睹其状，盖木、客、山、○、之类也。赣县西北十五里，有古塘，名余公塘，上有大梓树，可二十围，树老中空，有山都窠。宋元嘉元年，县治民袁道训道虚兄弟二人，伐倒此树。取窠还家。山都见形谓二人曰：“我处荒野，何豫汝事！巨木可用，岂可胜数？树有我窠，故伐倒之。今当焚汝宇，以报汝之无道。”至二更中，内外屋上一时火起，合宅荡尽。御览八百八十四广记三百二十四

南康雩都县沿江西出，广记引作跨江南出去县三里，名梦口，有穴、状如石室，名梦口穴。四字赋注引有旧传：尝有神鸡，色如好金，出此穴中，奋翼迥翔长鸣，响见之，辄飞入穴中，因号此石为金鸡石。已上略见类聚九十昔有人耕此山侧，望见鸡出游戏，有一长人操弹弹之，鸡遥见便飞入穴，弹丸正着穴上，丸径六尺许，下垂蔽穴，犹有间隙，不复容人。又有人乘船从下流还县，未至此崖数里，有一人通身黄衣，担两笼黄瓜，求寄载，因载二字御览引有之。黄衣人乞食，船主与之盘酒。二字广记引有食讫，船适至崖下。船主乞瓜，此人不与，仍唾盘上，径上崖，直入石中。船主初甚忿之，见其入石，始知神异，取向食器视之，见盘上唾，悉是黄金。珠林二十八广记四百御览八百十一又三百八十七事类赋注九 案亦见今本任昉述异记然甚简略不如此文详尽
芦塘有鲛鱼，五日一化，或为美异妇人，或为男子，至于变乱尤多。郡人相戒，故不敢有害心，鲛亦不能为计。后为雷电杀之，此塘遂涸。御览七十四

豫章郡有卢松屯，郡人二字广记引有罗根生于此屯傍垦荒种瓜，又于旁立一神坛。瓜始引蔓，清晨行之，忽见坛上有新板墨书，曰：“此是神地所游处，不得停止，种殖可速去。”根生拜谢跪咒曰：“窃疑屯人利此熟地生苗，容或假托神旨，以见驱斥，审是神教，愿更朱书赐报。”明早往看，向板犹存，悉以朱代墨，御览九百七十八根生谢而去也。广记二百九十四 章按县西有赤城，周三十里，一峰特高，可三百余丈。晋泰元中，有外国人白道猷居于此山，山神屡遣狼，怪形异声往恐怖之，道猷自若。山神乃自诣之云：“法师威德严重，今推此山相与，弟子更卜所托。”道猷曰：“君是何神？居此几时？今若必去，当去何所？”答云：“弟子夏王之子，居此千余年，寒石山是家舅所住，某且往寄憩，将来欲还会稽山庙。”临去遗信，赠三奁香，又躬来别，执手恨然。鸣鞞响角，淩空而逝。广记二百九十四

和州历阳沦为湖。先是有书生遇一老姥，姥待之厚，生谓姥曰：“此县门石龟眼血出，此地当陷为湖。”姥后数往候之。门使问姥，姥具以告。吏遂以朱点龟眼。姥见，遂走上北山，城遂陷。类林杂说十 出海口北行六十里，至腾屿之南溪，有淡水，清澈照底，有蟹焉：筐大如笠，脚长三足。宋元嘉中，章安县民屠虎取此蟹食之，肥美过常。虎其夜梦一少妪语之曰：“汝啖我，知汝寻被啖不？”屠氏明日出行，为虎所食，余家人殡瘗之，虎又发棺啖之，肌体无遗。此水今犹有大蟹，莫敢复犯。御览九百四十二
